author: @vivisector3489
text: Pretty impressive scanner, I feel like they've come a long way in relatively short time. Maybe you could do a comparison between what can be achieved with photogrammetry and the scanner? Also, the sound is so much better now, did you get a new mic?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i tired photogrammetry  while ago - i wasn't impressed tbh - but maybe that as come a long way too - good thought - cheers mate - and yes lol

author: @grendel1960a
text: I picked up a creality ferret Pro scanner in the black Friday sales and use the wifi bridge to connect it to my android tablet, and it certainly does the job ( though the android software doesnt seem quite as good as the windows version - or maybe i havent found all the settings yet for merging multiple scans) it certainly is a great bit of kit though.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i am finding it fun too mate

author: @SlyerFox666
text: That sure looked like an excellent scan, did you need to use any 3rd party software like mesh mixer to make any adjustments to the scan before print or was it just a case of letting the software do it all for you once you'd done the scan ?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: all I did was scan it, merge it and used mesh simplifiction - https://myminifactory.github.io/Fast-Quadric-Mesh-Simplification/ - to get it into tinkercad so I could add base and resize it

author: @craigmcmeechan5899
text: In my opinion, with any device from the past 3 years onwards s not so much processing power that'd the limiting factor, it's more software taking advantage of that processing power that's the limiting factor. Most iPhones and flagship android devices have processing power surpassing 3 Yr old intel desktop hardware.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that doesn't sound right mate - processing power is muscle to do a job and with a scan there are a lot of data points - software is more like technique - it's my guess both contribute - but all other things being equal the more processing power the better

replies.author: @craigmcmeechan5899
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  I appreciate your view, basic benchmarks will prove the oomph available in each processor *depending on metrics chosen to be measured can be compare side by side.  If you get down to the nitty gritty of API's available to each operating system. This is what separated actual applications from taking advantage of the power available.  A prime example would be 4k RAW video on iPads from 2018 onwards...   a standard computer cannot real time process this video.  You can skip through it at real time on a 5 year old ipad.  It comes down to Addressible Market for Device on Platform over Cost of Optimisation but I've no doubt taking full advantage of a Snapdragon or apple A chip will get the job done should rhebcoat of optimisation per user reach an acceptable level of cost

replies.author: @craigmcmeechan5899
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  also one final point is that you are correct about a point grid taking a lot of space for mathematical processing however on most mobile processors the CPU and GPU share the same memory address space (unified memory addressing) therefor can work on the same data in memory  real time.  There are the mathematical shortcomings of the ARM architecture to be considered but there are also gains of treating two 32 bit numbers in one 64 bitninstruction along with the massively parallel processing that can be achieved using the GPU
